input.background: A test of $H_0: p=0.21$ using a sample of $n=$ 57 was not significant $(P=0.103)$. Suppose the true prevalence in the population $p_1$ was 0.31.
input.question: What was the power of the test assuming a two-sided $\alpha$-level of 0.05?

output.answer:
$1-\beta=\Phi\left(\frac{\left|p_1-p_0\right| \sqrt{n}-z_{1-1} \sqrt{p_0 q_0}}{\sqrt{p_1 q_1}}\right)=$

$$
\Phi\left(\frac{|0.31-0.21| \sqrt{57}-1.96 \sqrt{(0.21)(0.79)}}{\sqrt{(0.31)(0.69)}}=\Phi(-0.09)=0.4641\right.
$$

[from Table B]. The power of the test under the stated conditions was about 46%.

sample_size.description: The size of the sample used in the hypothesis test.

null_hypothesis_proportion.description: The proportion under the null hypothesis, denoted as p in H0: p=0.21.

true_population_proportion.description: The true prevalence in the population, denoted as p1.

alpha_level.description: The significance level for the two-sided hypothesis test.

p_value.description: The p-value obtained from the hypothesis test, indicating the level of significance.

test_power.description: The power of the test, which is the probability of correctly rejecting the null hypothesis when the alternative hypothesis is true.
